text: YUPENG ZHANG: Hello, everyone.

Welcome back to our MOOC
on zero-knowledge proofs.

I'm Yupeng Zhang,
and today I'm going

to talk about polynomial
commitment schemes
based on error-correcting codes.

Recall that a common paradigm
to construct efficient SNARKs

for general circuits is to
combine a polynomial commitment

scheme with an appropriate
polynomial interactive proof,

such as the PLONK IOP and
the interactive proofs,

as we've seen in
previous lectures.

Last time I started this section
on polynomial commitments

and talked about the classical
protocol of the KCG polynomial
commitment.

The protocol utilizes
the bilinear pairing

and has this trusted
setup step, to compute

the global parameters with
this structure and the trapdoor
in it.

And the key idea of
the scheme relies

on this polynomial
equation to compute
the quotient polynomial q of x.

And the scheme has a very small
group size and a very fast
verifier time in practice.

The proof only consists of a
single element in the group,

and the verifier only needs
to compute a single parent
equation.

In addition, I also talked
about other polynomial schemes

without trusted setup, that are
based on discrete log problem.

Those include bulletproofs,
hyrax, dory, and dark.

In this lecture,
we are going to see

a new class of
polynomial schemes
based on error-correcting codes.

And to give you
some motivations,

here I'm showing you the pros
and cons of these schemes
on this slide.

So on the positive
side, we are not

aware of any efficient
algorithms on quantum computers

to break the assumptions
of these schemes.

So the schemes are
plausibly post-quantum,

unlike those that are based
on discrete log problem.

In addition, the prover
time of these schemes

tend to be fast, because
they do not require any group
exponentiations.

The prover only computes
hash functions, Merkle trees,

and some field additions,
multiplications, and FFT.

Finally, the size of the global
parameters is very small,
and there is no trusted set up.

All we need to do
is to sample a hash

from a family of hash functions
and the size is constant.

But of course, these nice
properties come at a cost.

The first drawback is
that the schemes usually
have a very large proof size.

And as you will
see later, we are

talking about several
megabytes to tens
of megabytes in practice.

And another drawback is
that because of the lack

of the algebraic structure
these proofs do not

have this homomorphic
property, and they
are hard to aggregate--

in a similar way as
we've seen last time

in the variants of KZG
polynomial commitments
to support multiple evaluations.

So to understand
the schemes based

on our error-occuring
codes, here's
the plan of this lecture.
There will be three segments.

In the first segment, I'm going
to talk about some background
on error-occuring codes.

And with that background,
in the second segment,

I'm going to present the
polynomial commitment schemes
using any error-occuring codes.

And in the last
segment, I'm going

to talk about this
interesting line of work,

to build linear-time encodable
code based on expander graphs.

And the polynomial
commitment schemes

and zere-knowledge proof
schemes using this type of code

to have a linear
time prover in terms

of the number of field additions
and field multiplications.

So let's start with
the background.

The error-occuring
code is actually

a well-studied topic in the
area of information theory
and coding theory.

It can be used to correct
errors in the transmission

over the network,
and that is why it's
called error-occuring code.

An error-occuring code
encodes a message of size k

to a codeword of size n, where
n is strictly larger than k.

An important property of
the error-occuring code

is this concept of
a minimum distance.

So the distance
between two codewords

is the number of different
locations between the two

codewords, and this is
called Hamming distance.

And if you take the minimum of
the distance between any two

codewords and call
it delta, that

is the minimum distance of
this error-occuring code.

And this n, k, and delta are
three important parameters

of the code, and we really
call it n. k, delta code.
I know that there's--

another important parameter
is the alphabet of the code,

whether it's defined over
a binary or a finite field,

but I'm omitting this
parameter in this notation.

So to give you an example, the
simplest example you're using
is this repetition code.

And here I'm using an
example with a message

of size 2 over
the binary values,
and the code was size 6.

So essentially, we are repeating
each symbol three times
to encode the message.

So that's why we have the
encoding of 00 gives you all

zeros, and encoding
of 01 is 000111.

And similarly, you
have the encoding of 10
and the encoding of 11.

And that's all possible
messages of length 2
over the binary field.

So then it's not hard to see
that the minimum distance
of this code is actually 3.

Because if you take any two
codewords from these four

possible codewords, we have
at least three locations

between any two codewords
that are different.

And because this
nice property, we

can actually correct one
error during the transmission.

Suppose that because the noise
level it can only introduce

at most one error
during a transmission,

and the codeword we
receive is 010111.

Then, because of this distant
property of the repetition

code, there is only one message
whose codeword is one error

away from this
codeword-- that is, 01.
And the codeword is 000111.

And that is why we are sure that
the original message must be

01, even with such an error
during the transmission.

So that is the
original application
of the error-correcting code.

And the process to derive
this original message

from a received codeword is
called decoding algorithm.

And an interesting
thing to note here

is that in our polynomial
commitment scheme,

we are not going to use this
decoding algorithm at all.

So because of that,
we can actually

use error-correcting codes
without efficient decoding.

So next, I'm just going to
define some additional terms.

So we have this rate of an
error-correcting code as k over

n So essentially, it's
representing the ratio

of a meaningful message when
we have a codeword of size k--
size n, sorry.

So this is basically
measuring out of n symbols,

we only have k symbols
representing the message.

And this is a number
between 0 and 1,

and apparently we want it to
be as close to 1 as possible.

And we also have this term
called relative distance,

is basically the minimum
distance delta divided by n.

So this is the ratio
of the codeword

that is different between
any two codewords.

And again, this is a
number between 0 and 1.

And for the purpose
of error correction,

we also want it to be
as big as possible.

And using the same
example of a repetition

code, if we have a rate of 1
or a, meaning that we are going

to repeat each symbol a times,
then from the previous example,

we can see that the
distance of this code is a.

And therefore the
relative distance

is the delta over n, where
n equals to k times a.
So the distance is 1 over k.

And turns out this is
actually not very good,

because it decays as the
message size k increases.

So it's not an
asymptotically good code.

And as we said, we want both the
rate and the relative distance
to be as big as possible.

But unfortunately,
there is usually

a trade off between the rate
and the distance of the code.

Intuitively speaking,
increasing the rate

will actually decrease the
relative distance of the code.

And we have well-studied
bounds on the relationship

between the rate and the
relative distance of a code
under different settings.

So the last concept we
introduce is this linear code.

And the linear code is actually
the most common type of code
we are using in practice.

So for linear code, we have
an additional requirement

that any linear combination of
codewords is also a codeword.

And because of
this property, it's

equivalent to say that the
encoding algorithm can always

be represented as
a vector matrix

multiplication between message
m and a generator matrix of size
k times n.

And because of all these cool
techniques in linear algebra,

the linear code can be analyzed
to have a nice properties,

and that's why it is the
most common type of code
we use in practice.

And now the implication
of the linear code

is that the minimum
distance is the same

as the number of the
least number of non-zeros
for any non-zero codewords.

And we call this as the
weight of the codeword.

And this is actually not hard
to see because the distance is

defined as the Hamming distance
between any two codewords.

And because the code is
linear, the subtraction

of those two code words
is also a codeword.

So that is saying that the
number of different locations

directly implies
another codeword

with that many
non-zero elements.

So therefore the
minimum distance

between any two
codewords is just

equivalent to the minimum
weight of a non-zero codeword
for a linear code.

That's something
to keep in mind.

So finally, I'm
going to show you

this classic construction
of the linear code called
Reed-Solomon code.

And this is actually a
very important construction

in the literature and
has many applications

to build cryptographic
primitives,

such as the zero-knowledge proof
schemes of Ligero and STARK

and also for secret sharing
and other applications
in cryptography.

So here I'm defining
Reed-Solomon code

over a finite-field
module of prime number p.

And the encode algorithm
takes a size k message

and encode it as
a size n codeword.

So the way to do
it is we're going

to view the message as a unique
degree k minus 1 univariate
polynomial.

You can think of it as a
polynomial interpolation

on a fixed set of
public coins of size k.

And we are going to treat
each symbol of the message

as the valuation at
a predefined point,

and then we're going to
interpolate it and get
a unique degree, k minus 1.9.

Then the codeword is
simply the valuation
of this polynomial at n points.

Again, these are predefined
and public points.

For example, we can
use the root of unity.

And we're going to
evaluate at omega,

omega to the 2, all the way
to omega to the n, where omega
to the n equals 1 module p.

And this is actually
a linear code,

because the encoding
algorithm can

be represented as
a vector matrix

multiplication between the
message and the generator

matrix, which can be derived
from the Fourier matrix.

And there is some code
has a very nice distance.

The distance is actually
n minus k plus 1.
And this is very good.

And that is because, as we
said in the previous slides,

for linear code the
minimum distance is simply

the minimum number
of non-zero elements
of a non-zero codeword.

Then as each code
word is actually

the evaluation of a degree k
minus 1 univariate polynomial,
how many locations can be 0?
Well at most, k minus 1.

That is because a degree
k minus 1 polynomial
has at most, k minus 1 roots.

So therefore at least n
minus k plus 1 locations

are non-zero for a
non-zero message.

So that is why the
distance is this one.

And to give you a
concrete number,
let's say that n equals to 2k.

If we're expanding the message
by 2 and get a codeword n

equals to 2k, then the
rate is a constant 1/2.

That's actually pretty
good in practice.

Then by this derivation, the
relative distance is also 1/2,
is a constant.

So the distance is also
very good in practice.

So it turns out this is actually
the best you can achieve,

and so the Reed-Solomon
code is asymptotically good.

And finally, the encoding time
of Reed-Solomon code is order

of n times log n using the fast
Fourier transform algorithm--

FFT to evaluate this
polynomial at these end points
defined by the roots of unity.

So that's everything
I want to talk about
for error-occuring codes.

With this background, in the

I'm going to show you how to

So recall that this is a setting

and a

matrix structure

So here I'm showing you
that a matrix consisting

of the polynomial,

from f11 all the way

And I'm assuming that the number

And the reason why we are

as this equation.

So we're going to have
two indices, i and j,

ranging from 1 all the

And then compute this product
between the coefficients

evaluate that point
u in this way.

And the nice property
of this way of computing

is that the polynomial
evaluation f of u

can actually be
decomposed into two steps.

So in the first

u of size square root d with
the matrix of the coefficients
of the polynomial.

And the result of this

will give you a vector
of size square root d.

Then in the second

The result will
be a single value

that equals to the

And if you do the derivation

matrix multiplications,
you can see

that this computation
is exactly the same
as the equation at the bottom.

So I'm saying here
that evaluation

can be viewed as this two steps
of vector-matrix on occasion.

With this observation,
we can actually

reduce the polynomial
commitment scheme

to an argument for
vector-matrix product.

So if you're good with a
scheme, with a square root

the proof size,

just prove the computation

So in the first
step, we are going

of the polynomial.

And the result of that is a

So then the prover can just

And the verifier verifies that

And then you evaluate

And in this way, the

this vector of

So this is saying that if we

then we are going to have

scheme with a square root d

So in this way, we

So then the focus of the

So then the dimension

After encoding, we end up

And the commitment is

So recall that in

And the construction
is to compute

the hash of two

And this root is served as

And later the prover can

And the proof is generated
by traversing this tree

and returning all the
siblings along the path

And the verifier can

with only the information
from the siblings,

and the proof size
is only logarithm

So this is the Merkle tree.

And the way to commit
to this polynomial

is, first, we are going to

using the linear code.

After that we are
going to use the Merkle

tree to commit to this
encoded matrix column-wise.

We are going to view each column

and then build this Merkle
tree on top of this matrix

And using this commitment,

that we can actually open

The prover can actually send a

and then together with

And the verifier can verify

is actually indeed as
committed in the first place
by the proof.

So this is the commitment step

I'm describing.

And because of this
commitment step,

All we need to do is to

So the raw algorithm can be

And the first step is called

And the main goal

to test if the

codewords encoded row-wise.

And the reason for

But if the prover

A malicious prover could

They're not even

And again, using the

But a malicious

So that is the purpose of

trying to ensure that the

And the way to do

So first the verifier is going

The prover cannot change the

Any linear combination
